"Ever dance with the devil in the pale moonlight?" — Jack Nicholson's Joker in Batman (1989) made this line iconic, though its meaning was never fully explained within the film.
The character of Batman has generated more peer-reviewed academic papers than any other comic book superhero, analyzing topics from psychology to political philosophy.
Batman's influence on the superhero genre is incalculable — he proved that a hero didn't need powers to be compelling, paving the way for characters like Iron Man, Green Arrow, and the Punisher.

"I made a promise on the grave of my parents that I would rid this city of the evil that took their lives." — Batman's fundamental oath, the bedrock upon which every version of the character is built.
Batman: Earth-Two, the Golden Age version, eventually married Catwoman and had a daughter, Helena Wayne, who became the Huntress.
The Batman: Knightquest saga that followed Knightfall showed Jean-Paul Valley (Azrael) as a progressively more violent and unhinged Batman, proving that the cowl requires Bruce Wayne's discipline.
Batman: Prodigal (1995) featured Dick Grayson temporarily becoming Batman after Jean-Paul Valley was defeated, showing the first successful succession of the Batman identity.
"There's nothing wrong with you that I can't fix with my hands." — Batman's menacing promise to a criminal captures his preference for direct, physical confrontation over weapons.
The Batman/Teenage Mutant Ninja Turtles crossover comics unexpectedly became fan favorites, blending Gotham's dark streets with the Turtles' sewer-dwelling adventures.
Batman: Hush Returns and Batman: Heart of Hush expanded on the original Hush storyline, with Heart of Hush by Paul Dini being particularly praised for its emotional depth.
"That mask — it's not to hide who I am. It's to create what I am." — Batman explaining that the cowl transforms him from a man into something more, a creature of the night.
The 1943 Batman serial starring Lewis Wilson was the first live-action portrayal of Batman, introducing concepts like the Batcave and Alfred's thin appearance that were later adopted by the comics.
Robert Lowery played Batman in the 1949 serial Batman and Robin, becoming the second actor to play the character on screen.
Batman's fear of bats, established in many modern origin stories, became the very weapon he turned against criminals — transforming his deepest terror into his greatest strength.
"Why bats, Master Wayne?" "Bats frighten me. It's time my enemies shared my dread." — Alfred and Bruce Wayne in Batman Begins, explaining the origin of Batman's symbol.
The Bat-family operates out of multiple headquarters in modern comics, including the Batcave, the Clock Tower (Oracle's base), and various safe houses hidden throughout Gotham.
Batman's influence extends to real-world crimefighting — the character has inspired community watch programs, anti-bullying campaigns, and charity organizations worldwide.
"It doesn't matter who we are. What matters is our plan." — Bane's philosophical declaration in The Dark Knight Rises, spoken to a man aboard a plane before a spectacular aerial hijacking.
The aerial sequence opening The Dark Knight Rises, where Bane hijacks a CIA plane mid-flight, was performed practically with real stunt performers and IMAX cameras.
Batman: Shadow of the Bat was a companion series that ran from 1992 to 2000, featuring stories that explored the darker corners of Gotham alongside the main Batman title.
"If I have to have a past, then I prefer it to be multiple choice." — The Joker in The Killing Joke, embracing ambiguity about his origin in a way that has defined the character ever since.
Batman's cape has been shown to contain 50 pounds of weighted fabric in some comic versions, allowing him to use it as a weapon for stunning opponents.
The Batsuit's cowl traditionally contains communications equipment, a voice modulator, detective vision lenses, and recording devices.
Adam West's Batman famously carried Bat-Shark-Repellent Spray in the 1966 film, one of many absurd gadgets from the utility belt that has been referenced and parodied for decades.
"Holy [everything], Batman!" — Burt Ward's Robin made this exclamation format so famous in the 1966 series that it became permanently associated with the character and the show's campy tone.
Batman and the Outsiders (1983) was a team comic where Batman left the Justice League to form his own squad of lesser-known heroes, including Black Lightning, Katana, and Metamorpho.
Batman has consistently been ranked as the most popular superhero in multiple polls worldwide, frequently surpassing Superman, Spider-Man, and other major characters.

"You have nothing, nothing to threaten me with. Nothing to do with all your strength." — The Joker to Batman in The Dark Knight, recognizing that Batman's refusal to kill is simultaneously his strength and his limitation.
Batman: Haunted Knight collected three Jeph Loeb/Tim Sale Halloween specials — Fears, Madness, and Ghosts — each retelling classic stories through a Batman lens.
The Joker has been killed and resurrected multiple times in DC Comics, making him nearly as persistent and unkillable as Batman himself.
Batman: Legends of the Dark Knight (1989–2007) was an anthology series featuring standalone Batman stories by different creative teams, allowing for experimental and diverse interpretations.
"I respect the mind's power over the body. It's why I do what I do." — Batman on the mental discipline required to push the human body beyond its assumed limits every single night.
